overall_feedback: This paper investigates COM formation in Jupiter's circumplanetary disk through thermal processing and UV irradiation of icy particles. While the physical model is well-constructed, the paper contains several serious methodological and interpretive issues. Most critically, the results section contains claims that are not logically supported by the described simulations, causal relationships are inverted or asserted without justification, selective reporting of favorable parameter regimes raises concerns about cherry-picking, and there is a duplicated paragraph in the introduction. The interpretation of particle trajectory data appears to conflate correlation with causation in several places, and some quantitative claims about COM formation efficiency appear to be stated as certainties without adequate statistical or physical justification.

comments.title: Duplicated paragraph in the Introduction
comments.quote: The formation and transport of COMs in protoplanetary disks (PPDs) have been extensively investigated \citep{Wa14,Ben24,Benest_Couzinou_2025}. In contrast, comparatively little attention has been devoted to the synthesis, alteration, or survival of COMs within circumplanetary disks (CPDs), despite these being the environments in which the satellites of gas giants are thought to form \citep{canup2002, canup2006, Ronnet2017, anderson2021}.
comments.explanation: The entire paragraph beginning with 'The formation and transport of COMs in protoplanetary disks (PPDs)...' is duplicated verbatim in the Introduction. This appears to be an editing error, but it also means the logical flow of the introduction is disrupted, and it is unclear which version (if either differs) represents the authors' intended argument.

comments.title: Inverted causal claim: COM formation driving particle transport
comments.quote: These results suggest that COM formation is the primary driver of particle transport in the CPD.
comments.explanation: This claim inverts cause and effect. The model tracks particles drifting through the CPD due to gas drag and aerodynamic forces; COM formation is a consequence of particles passing through certain temperature/irradiation zones, not a cause of their transport. The particle dynamics are governed by the Stokes number, gas velocity, and pressure gradients (Equations 8-9), none of which depend on whether COMs have formed. There is no feedback mechanism described in the model by which COM formation would alter particle trajectories.

comments.title: Unjustified claim of 100% COM conversion efficiency
comments.quote: The data shows that because the trajectories intersect the COM zone, the conversion of ice to COMs is 100\% efficient for all particle sizes.
comments.explanation: Passing through a temperature zone does not guarantee 100% conversion efficiency. The laboratory experiments by Bossa et al. (2008) show that reactants begin to react above 80 K and are 'completely consumed by 130 K,' but this occurs under specific laboratory timescales and conditions. The residence time of particles in the thermal processing zone (shown to be as short as <1 yr for 1 cm particles at t_0=150 kyr) may be insufficient for complete conversion. The paper does not model reaction kinetics or demonstrate that the residence times are long enough relative to the reaction timescales to justify 100% efficiency.

comments.title: Selective reporting and exclusion of unfavorable results (p-hacking concern)
comments.quote: We focus our analysis exclusively on the $\alpha = 10^{-3}$ and $R_c = 50$ cases where significant residence times were observed, as these provide the most robust evidence for our hypothesis, while omitting the non-significant results from the high-turbulence $\alpha = 10^{-2}$ scenarios to clarify the presentation.
comments.explanation: This is a clear case of selective reporting that biases toward confirming the authors' hypothesis. Excluding parameter regimes that do not support the hypothesis (α=10^{-2}) while only presenting those that do (α=10^{-3}) constitutes cherry-picking. A rigorous study should present all results and discuss under which conditions the hypothesis holds or fails. The authors acknowledge that α=10^{-2} produces no COMs via irradiation, which is itself an important result that should be given equal weight.

comments.title: Selective radial bin reporting based on statistical significance
comments.quote: Note that we have adjusted the radial bins to only include those that showed a p-value < 0.05 for inward migration.
comments.explanation: Filtering data points post-hoc based on statistical significance and only reporting those that pass a threshold is a form of p-hacking. The selection criterion ('inward migration') is the outcome of interest, so excluding bins that don't show it and then claiming the remaining bins demonstrate inward migration is circular. The total number of bins tested, the multiple comparisons problem, and what statistical test was used are not described.

comments.title: Contradictory claims about 1 cm particle residence times and COM formation
comments.quote: The figure demonstrates that because 1~cm particles migrate faster at $t_0 = 150$~kyr, they are more likely to form COMs than the 1~$\mu$m particles, as their residence time in the zone is effectively infinite relative to the timestep.
comments.explanation: This claim directly contradicts the text in the same section which states: 'None of the 1~cm particles released at $t_0$ = 150~kyr remain in the stability region for more than 1~yr, which corresponds to the temporal resolution of the iterative scheme of our model.' A residence time at or below the temporal resolution cannot be described as 'effectively infinite' — it means the model cannot resolve the actual residence time, which could be much shorter. Furthermore, faster migration should reduce residence time in any given zone, making COM formation less likely, not more.

comments.title: Incorrect physical interpretation: particle decoupling causing CPD depletion
comments.quote: As the Stokes number of the particles increases, we observe a corresponding decrease in the local gas density, suggesting that the decoupling of large particles is the primary cause of the CPD's depletion over time.
comments.explanation: The CPD's gas density decreases over time because of the exponentially declining accretion rate (Equation 1), not because of particle decoupling. The Stokes number increases as gas density decreases (St = t_s × Ω_K, where t_s ∝ 1/ρ_g), so the correlation is expected but the causation is reversed. Dust particles constitute only a small fraction of the disk mass (metallicity of 2.45×10^{-3}), so their decoupling cannot drive the depletion of the gas disk.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about COM formation altering disk thermal structure
comments.quote: At $t_0$ = 100 $\mathrm{kyr}$ and 150 $\mathrm{kyr}$, the shift of the thermal processing region closer to Jupiter is driven by the conversion of NH$_3$:CO$_2$ ices into COMs, which alters the disk's thermal structure.
comments.explanation: The model described in Section 2.1 computes the CPD thermal structure from the accretion rate, viscosity, and radiative transfer — there is no described feedback mechanism by which chemical conversion of trace ice species (at metallicity 2.45×10^{-3}) would alter the disk's thermal structure. The thermal processing region moves inward because the disk cools as the accretion rate declines exponentially (Equation 1), shifting the 80-260 K zone to smaller radii.

comments.title: Misinterpretation of irradiation threshold crossing
comments.quote: Assuming the particles are primarily composed of CH$_3$OH ice, the data suggests that the sublimation of ice at 105 K is caused by the particles reaching the lower irradiation threshold.
comments.explanation: Sublimation at 105 K is a thermal process that occurs when particles reach regions at that temperature. It cannot be 'caused by' reaching an irradiation threshold. These are independent physical processes. The temporal coincidence of reaching the irradiation threshold and the sublimation temperature does not imply causation — both occur as the particle drifts inward into warmer, more shielded regions.

comments.title: Caption claim contradicts physical model for UV irradiation
comments.quote: The fact that dashed curves indicate CH$_3$OH in the vapor phase confirms that UV irradiation is the sole cause of ice loss, as the thermal thresholds are not reached.
comments.explanation: If CH3OH is in the vapor phase, this indicates thermal sublimation (at 105 K as stated in Section 2.3), not UV-driven destruction. UV photolysis breaks molecular bonds but does not cause sublimation. The statement conflates two distinct processes. Moreover, if thermal thresholds 'are not reached,' then the particles haven't reached 105 K, which contradicts the claim that CH3OH is in the vapor phase due to sublimation.

comments.title: Inconsistency in reported irradiation thresholds between text sections
comments.quote: Two irradiation thresholds are indicated: a lower fluence of $8.64\times10^{17}$ photons~cm$^{-2}$ and a higher fluence of $4.32\times10^{19}$ photons~cm$^{-2}$.
comments.explanation: In Section 2.3, the paper states that Tenelanda-Osorio et al. (2022) show COM formation at fluences as low as ~9×10^{15} photons cm^{-2}, with 8.64×10^{17} described as 'the upper bound of the laboratory conditions rather than a formation threshold.' Yet in the Results section, 8.64×10^{17} is used as a 'lower' threshold for COM formation. This is inconsistent — if 8.64×10^{17} is the upper bound of explored conditions (not a threshold), it should not be used as a minimum requirement for COM formation in the model.

comments.title: Duplicated Discussion section header
comments.explanation: The Discussion section appears twice with the same label. The first version contains the selective reporting statement, while the second version presents a different opening paragraph about thermal processing dominating over photochemistry. This duplication suggests the paper was not properly edited before submission, and it is unclear which version represents the authors' intended discussion.
